mappings.rationaleAI: Directly covers the life and teachings of Confucius (B.1.b — origin in the life and teachings of Confucius, the first Sage) and the early Confucian school through Mencius and Xunzi (B.1.c), foundational to understanding Confucianism as both religion and philosophy (B.3).

Directly addresses the legendary founder of Taoism and the Tao Te Ching (C.1.a — origin and early developments of Taoism), which articulates the Tao, wu wei, and the foundational Taoist philosophical framework; also relevant to C.3 (Taoism as religion and philosophy).

The Tang dynasty period was central to Chinese religious history: the Confucian-Buddhist-Daoist synthesis (A.2.c — Buddhist dominance and Taoist cults; B.1.f — Confucianism during Buddhist ascendancy), woodblock printing affecting textual transmission, and tributary relations spreading these traditions to Korea and Japan.

Comprehensive coverage of Confucianism including its history from Confucius through Han state orthodoxy (B.1.d), introduction to Korea and Japan (B.1.e), role during Buddhist ascendancy (B.1.f), Neo-Confucian development (B.1.g), responses to Western challenge (B.1.h), and contemporary aspects (B.1.i), as well as Confucian literature and its status as religion and philosophy (B.2–B.3).

Directly and comprehensively covers Shinto (E.2) — its history (E.2.a/E.1), characteristics of primitive Shinto including guardian shrines and shamans (E.2.b), scriptural literature including Kojiki and Nihon Shoki (E.2.c), basic beliefs and doctrines (E.2.d), ritual practices and institutions (E.2.e), and connects to Japanese religious art and symbolism (E.3).

Comprehensive coverage of Taoism in all dimensions of the outline: history from Laozi and Zhuangzi origins (C.1.a) through Han-era developments (C.1.b), interaction with Buddhism (C.1.c), Tang-Song internal developments (C.1.d), through to the modern period (C.1.e); also covers Taoist literature (C.2) and Taoism as religion and philosophy (C.3).

Covers the philosophical dimensions of both Confucianism (B.3) and Taoism (C.3) as part of its survey of East and South Asian philosophical traditions, addressing their ethical, cosmological, and metaphysical concerns as philosophical systems.

Directly covers the establishment of Confucianism as state orthodoxy under Han Emperor Wu (B.1.d — Confucianism as Han state orthodoxy, eclectic tendencies, skeptical reactions) and the early formation of Chinese civilization including ancestor worship and cosmological beliefs (A.2.b context).

Provides comparative historical framework for the development of Chinese, Korean, and Japanese religious traditions (A.2 broadly), including ancestor worship, Axial Age religious formation (corresponding to A.2.b), and the broad historical trajectory of East Asian religions.

Buddhism's dominance in Chinese religious history (A.2.c — Buddhist Way and Taoist-inspired cults, 1st–16th century; C.1.c — Taoism's interaction with Buddhism), its introduction into Korea (D.1) and Japan (E.1.b), and its interaction with Confucianism and Taoism are core topics within this article.

Mahayana Buddhism is the dominant form that spread through China (A.2.c), interacted with Taoism (C.1.c), and was introduced into Japan during the early historic period (E.1.b), making it directly relevant to sections covering the Buddhist Way's role in East Asian religion.

Directly covers the distinction between folk and literate religions in China (A.1), the syncretic integration of Buddhism, Confucianism, and Taoism (A.2.c, A.2.d), traditional cosmological concepts including Tian, qi, yin-yang, and ancestor veneration (A.3), ritual practices and institutions (A.4), and religious symbolism (A.5).

Provides comprehensive regional context for Chinese religion (A), Confucianism (B), Taoism (C), Korean religion (D), and Japanese religion (E), covering the Sinosphere's shared cultural heritage of Confucianism, Buddhism, and Taoism across China, Korea, Japan, and Mongolia.

Mao's Cultural Revolution represented the most radical political assault on Confucian ideology and Chinese religious traditions (A.2.d — effect of nationalism and secularism; B.1.h — varied responses to political developments; B.1.i — Confucianism today under communist governance).

The Tale of Genji reflects the Heian period's Buddhist-Shinto religious milieu (E.1.b — early historic and medieval Japan, introduction of Buddhism) including Buddhist themes of impermanence and mono no aware; connects to Japanese religious art and cultural symbolism (E.3).

Provides broad historical context for Chinese religious development (A.2), Korean religious history including Joseon dynasty religious policy (D.1), and early Japanese religious history (E.1), covering the Zhou, Han, Tang, Ming, Joseon, and Tokugawa periods relevant to these religious traditions.

Covers Korean religious history from ancient kingdoms through the Joseon dynasty's Neo-Confucian state orthodoxy to contemporary religious pluralism (D.1), directly addressing the history, beliefs, and practices of Korean religion including Confucianism, Buddhism, and Christianity.

Covers the emergence of early Chinese civilization including ancestor worship and cosmological beliefs (A.2.a) and the pre-dynastic institutions that formed the background for Confucianism (B.1.a — predynastic sage-emperors and founders of the first three dynasties).

Surveys Chinese Confucian, Daoist, and Buddhist philosophical thought as part of its non-Western coverage (B.3 — Confucianism as religion and philosophy; C.3 — Taoism as religion and philosophy), contextualizing East Asian philosophy within the global philosophical tradition.

Covers the Chinese scholar-painting ideal and East Asian printmaking traditions, relevant to Japanese religious art and symbolism (E.3), including the aesthetic traditions shaped by Shinto and Buddhist visual culture in East Asia.

The Western Christian missionary presence in East Asia is part of the modern period's impact on Chinese religion (A.2.d) and represents the intellectual and religious challenge of the West to which Confucianism had to respond (B.1.h).

The early modern period encompasses the Tokugawa era in Japan and late Ming-early Qing China, directly relevant to Confucian responses to Western intellectual challenge (B.1.h) and the later development of Taoism from the 14th century (C.1.e), as well as Japanese Neo-Confucian Shinto (E.1.c).
